query: The headless iOS app publishing pipeline for AI agents. complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Setting up a delivery pipeline for your agentic iOS projects
results.content:
The pipeline I use looks like this: a well-maintained `agents.md` and AI skills set the foundation locally. Planning mode ensures the agent's approach is sound before it writes a line of code. BugBot catches issues in pull requests that I might miss. Bitrise runs tests on every PR and archives plus uploads on every merge to main. And TestFlight delivers the result to my device automatically.

`agents.md`

Each piece reinforces the others. Without good local setup, the agent writes worse code. Without planning, it makes bad architectural decisions. Without BugBot and Bitrise, bugs slip through. Without automatic TestFlight uploads, the feedback loop is too slow to be useful. [...] To be clear: this pipeline doesn't catch everything. An agent can still write code that passes all tests but is architecturally questionable, and BugBot won't always flag it. You still need to review and think critically. But the combination of all these layers seriously cuts down the risk of shipping something broken — and that's the point. It's about reducing risk, not eliminating it.

If you're prototyping or just exploring an idea, you probably don't need all of this right away. But the moment you have real users depending on your app, this kind of pipeline pays for itself. Set it up once, iterate on your `agents.md` as you go, and you'll be able to move fast without sacrificing the quality your users expect.

`agents.md`

## Categories

## Post navigation

Previous post [...] I'm hoping to be a little more balanced than that...

Agentic engineering (aka vibe coding) is becoming more popular by the day. More and more developers are letting AI agents handle large parts of their iOS projects, and honestly, I get it. It's incredibly productive. But it comes with a real risk: when you hand off the coding to an agent, quality and architecture can degrade fast if you don't have the right guardrails in place.

In this post, I want to walk you through the pipeline I use to make sure that even though I do agentic engineering, my product quality stays solid (yes, it involves me reading the code and sometimes tweaking by hand).

results.title: Replit Docs
results.content:
### ​ Check health check timing

Before marking your published app as live, Replit sends an HTTP request to your app. If your homepage takes more than five seconds to respond, the health check can time out and publishing can fail at the final step. If startup is slow, ask Agent to reduce startup work, move expensive work behind a user action, or cache data where appropriate.

## ​ If publishing succeeds but the public URL is broken

If Preview works and publishing succeeds, but the public URL is broken, compare Preview and production behavior. Check: [...] ## ​ What you’ll achieve

By the end of this page, you should know whether the issue is in your app, publishing setup, production Secrets, deployment type, port configuration, or network/DNS access.

## ​ Start with Preview

Before publishing, verify that the app runs correctly in the Project Editor. Select Run at the top of the Project Editor and open Preview. If Preview does not display your app with a `replit.dev` URL, fix the app before publishing. Ask Agent to diagnose Preview first: If Preview is broken, publishing will not fix it.

## ​ If Preview works but publishing fails

When Preview works but publishing fails, check publishing-specific setup.

### ​ Check logs [...] ### ​ Check logs

Open the Logs tab in the Publishing pane. Look for stack traces, missing packages, failed commands, missing environment variables, or startup errors. Paste the relevant error into Agent:

### ​ Check run and build commands

Common mistakes include typos, incorrect flags, wrong entry files, or commands that only work locally. Review commands in the Publishing pane. If your app needs a build command and a start command, confirm both are correct.

### ​ Check production Secrets

results.title: Building iOS Apps with AI Agents: The Practitioner's Guide
results.content:
### XcodeBuildMCP: 59 Tools for Headless iOS Development

XcodeBuildMCP wraps `xcodebuild`, `xcrun simctl`, and LLDB into 59 structured MCP tools. It works without Xcode running — the entire build-test-debug cycle operates headlessly via Apple’s command-line tools.

Installation options:

```
# Option 1: Via npx (recommended — always uses latest version)     \    \   XCODEBUILDMCP_SENTRY_DISABLED = true  \      # Option 2: Via Homebrew (pinned version, manual updates)       \    \   XCODEBUILDMCP_SENTRY_DISABLED = true  \    # Option 3: Project-scoped (omit -s user)     \   XCODEBUILDMCP_SENTRY_DISABLED = true  \      
``` [...] For experienced agent users adding iOS to their workflow:

 MCP transforms the iOS build loop. Before MCP, agents wrote Swift but could not verify it compiled. With XcodeBuildMCP, the agent writes code, builds it, reads structured errors, fixes them, and runs tests — autonomously.
 Three runtimes serve different needs. Claude Code CLI for deep agentic sessions, Codex CLI for headless batch work, Xcode 26.3 native agents for quick inline fixes without leaving the IDE.
 Hook infrastructure carries over. Your existing PostToolUse formatters, PreToolUse blockers, and test runner hooks work identically for iOS projects with minor path adjustments.

For team leads evaluating AI-assisted iOS development: [...] Agent weaknesses with SpriteKit: - Performance-sensitive game loops (the agent adds unnecessary work per frame) - Complex physics simulations (custom physics beats SKPhysicsBody for precision) - Particle effect tuning (visual, requires iteration)

### Metal

Apps using it: amp97, Water, Starfield Destroyer

Metal is the framework where agents struggle most. The GPU programming model is fundamentally different from CPU-side Swift, and agents cannot verify visual output.

```
 ## Metal Rules -  -  -  -  -  -  
```

What to include in CLAUDE.md for Metal projects: - The Uniforms struct definition (shared between Swift and MSL) - The render pipeline state setup pattern - Buffer indices and their purposes - Which shaders exist and what each does - Known precision issues (half vs. float)

results.title: Your AI Can Submit Apps to the App Store Now. No, Seriously. - DEV Community
results.content:
For indie devs and small teams, this overhead is brutal. You finally finish building your feature, you're excited, and then you spend the next hour clicking through dashboards and resizing PNGs. It kills momentum. It kills joy. It kills the part of your brain that actually enjoys making software.

And don't even get me started on TestFlight. The workflow of "upload build, wait for processing, add testers, write test notes, submit" is simple in theory and annoying in practice. Every. Single. Time.

## Enter app-publish-mcp

So I found app-publish-mcp last week, and I'll be honest — I was skeptical. Another MCP server? Cool. But this one actually solves a real problem I have.
